coredata.dc:description: Advanced oxidation process (AOP) through the photocatalytic activation of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) has attracted much attention in wastewater treatment. Herein, a metal-free carbon quantum dots (CQDs) functioned polymer carbon nitride (PCN) was synthesized and used as an efficient photocatalyst for PMS activation toward bisphenol A (BPA) degradation under visible-light irradiation. The incorporation of CQDs in PCN endowed it with enlarged surface area, enhanced light absorption and improved charge separation, which were advantageous to photocatalytic activation of PMS. As a result, the CQDs functionalized carbon nitride exhibited an enhanced performance in photocatalytic activation of PMS toward BPA removal. The influencing factors such as catalyst dosage, PMS concentration, pH value, and anion additive on the photocatalytic performance were well examined. The catalyst exhibited a good cyclic stability. Furthermore, the reactive species and intermediates involved in BPA degradation were also investigated to elucidate the possible mineralization pathway of BPA. This work provides a facile synthetic strategy to prepare metal-free catalyst for photocatalytic activation of PMS in wastewater treatment.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic activation of peroxymonosulfate by carbon quantum dots functionalized carbon nitride for efficient degradation of bisphenol A under visible-light irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The enhanced catalytic performance for BPA removal over CQDs modified carbon nitride was achieved through efficient photocatalytic activation of PMS under visible light irradiation.

Advanced oxidation process (AOP) through the photocatalytic activation of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) has attracted much attention in wastewater treatment. Herein, a metal-free carbon quantum dots (CQDs) functioned polymer carbon nitride (PCN) was synthesized and used as an efficient photocatalyst for PMS activation toward bisphenol A (BPA) degradation under visible-light irradiation. The incorporation of CQDs in PCN endowed it with enlarged surface area, enhanced light absorption and improved charge separation, which were advantageous to photocatalytic activation of PMS. As a result, the CQDs functionalized carbon nitride exhibited an enhanced performance in photocatalytic activation of PMS toward BPA removal. The influencing factors such as catalyst dosage, PMS concentration, pH value, and anion additive on the photocatalytic performance were well examined. The catalyst exhibited a good cyclic stability. Furthermore, the reactive species and intermediates involved in BPA degradation were also investigated to elucidate the possible mineralization pathway of BPA. This work provides a facile synthetic strategy to prepare metal-free catalyst for photocatalytic activation of PMS in wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution caused by refractory organics such as plastic and pharmaceuticals has become an environmental issue seriously affecting human health and ecosystem [1–3]. Owing to their rather stable molecular structures, high toxicity and low biodegradability, these organics are difficult to be completely removed by conventional treatment methods. For instance, as one of the most active endocrine-disrupting compounds, bisphenol A (BPA) can mimic the functions of estradiol, disrupt normal endocrine function and cause adverse human health effects [4]. Recently, the development of advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) based on the generated highly reactive oxygen species (ROS) have gained extensive attention for efficient wastewater treatment. Among those, persulfate-based advanced oxidation processes exhibit significant advantages in destroying and mineralize recalcitrant organics due to the generation of a sulfate radical (SO4•-) with high oxidation potential (2.7 ~ 3.1 V) and long life period (30 ~ 40 μs) [5,6]. As a type of frequently-used persulfate, peroxymonosulfate (PMS) can be activated by different technologies [7–10], including thermal treatment, ultrasonic irradiation, catalysis and photocatalysis. Among them, visible-light-driven photocatalytic activation has a good application perspective owing to its high efficiency and potential utilization of solar energy.

Various kinds of photocatalysts, in particular the ones contain Mn, Co and Fe have been extensively examined to activate PMS. For example, ZnFe2O4, CuBi2O4, Co(OH)2 decorated WO3 and iron oxide doped MOFs have been found effective to activate PMS and subsequently eliminate organic compound in water [11–17]. However, most of them suffered from the issue of metal leaching that potentially possesses the risks of catalyst deactivation and secondary pollution. Consequently, it is highly desirable to develop metal-free photocatalysts for PMS activation toward the removal of organic compounds in the wastewater [18].

Recently, polymer carbon nitride (PCN) has been developed as a metal-free photocatalyst for water splitting, CO2 conversion, organic transformation and wastewater purification [19–25]. Owing to its unique electronic structure and excellent chemical stability, PCN is also a promising candidate for photocatalytic activation of PMS under visible-light irradiation [26–30]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic efficiency toward PMS activation by pristine PCN is unsatisfactory, mainly due to the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and the lack of sufficient reactive sites for PMS activation [31,32]. To overcome these issues, metal (such as Ag, Co and Mn), metal oxide, metal phosphide, or metal phosphate modified PCN have been synthesized and exhibited improved photocatalytic performance [33–41], but the leaching of metals in these systems is still unavoidable. Recently, carbon nanomaterials, in particular carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely employed to advance semiconductors with improved conductivity and electron transfer behavior by taking advantage of its unique electronic properties [42–46]. For instance, CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic performance in the degradation of organic pollutants [47,48]. Additionally, the electron enriched CQDs in semiconductor can be beneficial to activate PMS for ROS production under light irradiation. To this end, the CQDs modified PCN (PCNC) could be a promising metal-free photocatalyst for PMS activation toward organics degradation (Scheme 1 ).

Herein, the coupling of PCN nanosheets with CQDs was conducted via a facile thermal polymerization strategy. The obtained metal-free catalysts were employed for photocatalytic activation of PMS toward BPA degradation under visible-light (λ greater than 420 nm) irradiation, and the optimized catalyst showed a good performance for the removal of BPA. The structural, optical and electrochemical properties of the catalysts were also investigated. The influencing factors such as catalyst dosage, PMS concentration, pH value, and anion additive on the catalytic performance were well examined. Additionally, electron spin resonance trapping measurement, scavenger quenching experiment and intermediate identification were carried out to reveal the possible reaction mechanism for BPA mineralization.

Potassium peroxysulfate, bisphenol A, furfuryl alcohol (FFA), tertiary butanol (TBA) and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Urea, citric acid, sodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate, (EDTA-Na), and methanol (MeOH) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). 5, 5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethy l-4-piperidinol (TMP) were provided by J&K Chemical Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without any further purification, and all aqueous solutions were prepared with the deionized water.

CQDs were synthesized by a modified method according to the reported [49]. Typically, 7.5 g of citric acid and 2.5 g of urea were dissolved in 37.5 mL deionized water. The mixture was put into a 100 mL autoclave and undergone the hydrothermal process at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained dark brown turbid solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 20 min to remove the bulk particles. The size of the retained CQDs is about 10 nm (Fig. S1), and the CQDs solution was kept at 4 °C for subsequent synthetic procedures.

A common thermal polymerization method was used to prepare PCNC. Typically, 10 g of urea and a certain volume of CQDs solution were mixed in 30 mL deionized water. Then the blackish green mixture was transferred to a 50 mL crucible and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The obtained dark grey solid was calcined at 550 °C for 3 h with the heating rate of 5 °C min−1 in a muffle oven. The resultant samples modified with different amount of CQDs (25 uL, 50 uL, 75 uL, 100 uL) were denoted as PCNC-1, PCNC-2, PCNC-3 and PCNC-4, respectively. PCN was also obtained via the same process without adding the CQDs.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out over Bruker D8 Advance XRD equipment with Cu Ka radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was carried out on a NICOLET IS50 (Thermo SCIENTIFIC). The morphology and structure was analyzed by field scanning electron microscope (SEM, Nova NanoSEM 230) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TECNAI G2 F20). The data of Brunauer-Emmett-Teller specific surface area was obtained from Micromeritics ASAP 2020 apparatus. X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was recorded on a PHI Quantum 2000 XPS with the C1s peak (284.6 eV) as a reference. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS) was performed on a Cary 5000 of Agilent Technologies. Photoluminescence spectrum was recorded on a modular fluorescence spectrometer (Fluorolog-3 Horiba). The photoelectrochemical measurement was carried out using a potentiostat (Bio-Logic SP-150) with three-electrode configuration, which included the working electrode (PCN or PCNC-2 film), a platinum-mesh counter electrode and Ag/AgCl reference electrode. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was collected on a Bruker model ESA A300 spectrometer.

The catalytic degradation of BPA in the presence of PMS was performed under visible light obtained from a 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE300D/UV, Perfect Light technology) with a 420 nm cut-off filter, and the light intensity (420 ~ 780 nm) is 350 mW/cm2. The reaction temperature was maintained at 20.0 °C through a circulating water bath. In the catalytic activity test, 50 mg of catalyst and 150 mL of BPA (20 mg/L) solution were mixed in a reactor. The initial pH value of BPA was about 6.7. The pH value of BPA solution was adjusted by 0.1 M H2SO4 or 0.1 M KOH. The mixture was stirred for 0.5 h in darkness to achieve the adsorption–desorption equilibrium between BPA and catalyst. Then 30 mg/L of PMS was added to the mixture and the system was exposed to visible light in the meantime. During the reaction, 0.5 mL of the reaction suspension was sampled and immediately filtrated by a 0.22 μm polyethersulfone membrane at each regular interval for detection, with the addition of 0.5 mL Me
OH for the quenching of the residual PMS. All the control experiments were operated under similar conditions.

The concentration of BPA in solution was determined by HPLC (1260 infinity Agilent Technologies) analysis at 224 nm. The temperature of the column was set to 30 °C, the mobile phase was comprised of methanol and deionized water (volumetric ratio: 70:30) with a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min, and the injection volume was set as 10 uL. The intermediates in the reaction were detected by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry technology (TSQ Quantum Access MAX/TSQ Quantum Access MAX Thermo Fisher Scientific). The total organic carbon (TOC) in the BPA solution without adding methanol as the quencher for PMS was analyzed with a Shimadzu TOC-VCPH analyzer.

Fig. 1 a displays the XRD patterns of the synthesized catalysts. The peak at 27.5° corresponds to the (002) of carbon nitride originated from the interlayer-stacking of conjugated aromatic system, and the peak at 13.0° is related to the in-plane structural packing motif of tri-s-triazine units in carbon nitride [49,50]. These two peaks become broader and weaker with the increase of CQDs amount in the sample, indicating that the introduction of CQDs in carbon nitride matrix has induced structural distortion of the polymer. The chemical structure of the catalysts was examined by FT-IR spectrum. In Fig. 1b, the samples display similar FT-IR spectrum. The broad band at 2800 ~ 3600 cm−1 belongs to the N–H and O–H stretches, several vibration bands at 1200 ~ 1600 cm−1 could be attributed to the C-N stretching vibrations in the CN heterocycles, and the band at 804 cm−1 is related to the s-triazine ring mode [51,52]. These findings demonstrate that the chemical framework of carbon nitride can be well maintained after being modified by CQDs.

The morphology of the catalysts was imaged by SEM. In Fig. 2 a-b, a typical porous structure originated from nanosheets aggregation is observed for PCN and PCNC-2, in good agreement with the unique morphology of urea-derived carbon nitride [53–56]. As a result of structural distortion by CQDs incorporation, more abundant pores are disclosed for PCNC-2, which is further confirmed by the result of N2-sorption measurement. In Fig. S2, both of PCN and PCNC-2 exhibit a type H3 hysteresis loop at the relative pressure of 0.8 ~ 1.0, indicative of a mesoporous structure [47]. The specific surface area of PCNC-2 is 150 m2/g, higher than that of PCN (90 m2/g). The enlarged surface area will be beneficial for the adsorption of pollutant on the catalyst surface, which is confirmed by the BPA adsorption behavior over PCN and PCNC-2 (Fig. S3). Additionally, it could provide abundant active sites for catalytic reaction and facilitate mass transfer, which has been proved to be advantageous to the catalytic reaction [48]. PCNC-2 was further characterized by TEM and the results are shown in Fig. 2c-d. It consists of the overlapped nanosheets with porous structure, and the CQDs are embedded in carbon nitride matrix (Fig. 2c). The HRTEM image of PCNC-2 clearly displays a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm (Fig. 2d), characteristic of pristine CQDs [44]. These results illustrate that the CQDs have been successfully incorporated in PCN to form PCNC nanocomposite.

The chemical composition and state of the catalysts were characterized by XPS analysis. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. S4) show that both PCN and PCNC-2 mainly consist of C and N elements, with trace oxygen originated from water and oxygen in air. The atomic ratio of C/N is 0.83 for PCN, while it increases to 1.14 for PCNC-2 due to the incorporation of CQDs. In the C1s spectra (Fig. 3 a), there are three distinct peaks, including the one at 284.6 eV for graphitic carbon, the one at 286.3 eV for carbon boned to hydroxyl groups (C-OH), and the one at 287.9 eV for triazine ring carbon (N-C = N) [57]. The higher content of graphitic carbon in PCNC-2 could be ascribed to the incorporation of CQDs. In the N1s spectra (Fig. 3b), the band at 398 ~ 402 eV can be deconvoluted into three components, sp2-hybridized nitrogen in aromatic triazine rings (398.5 eV), tertiary nitrogen (399.8 eV) and amino groups (400.9 eV) [58]. As expected, the incorporation of CQDs into polymer matrix has not significantly altered the chemical structure of carbon nitride.

The optical properties of the catalysts were investigated by UV–Vis DRS spectroscopy. In Fig. 4 a, all the samples display a sharp absorption edge located at about 450 nm, which is associated with the excitation of electrons from the valence band to the conduction band of carbon nitride semiconductor. Although the modification of CQDs does not obviously affect the absorption edge, an enhanced light harvesting capability is obtained. The absorption shoulder at 400 ~ 800 nm could be due to the photon absorption of CQDs in the samples [49]. The Tauc plots obtained from the Kubelka-Munk function (the inset in Fig. 4a) reveal that the band gap energies of PCNC-2 and PCN are determined to be 2.57 and 2.70 eV, respectively [28,30]. These results indicate that the coupling of CQDs with PCN could realize an enhanced utilization of visible light.

Mott-Schottky plots were used to determine the flat band potential of semiconductor catalysts [59]. In Fig. 4b, the positive slope of the Mott-Schottky plots clearly shows n-type characteristics for PCN and PCNC-2. For n-type semiconductor, the conduction band (CB) locates at the position close to flat band, thus the conduction band (CB) edge potentials are ca. −1.33 and −0.96 V vs. RHE for PCN and PCNC-2, respectively. Likewise, the CB edge potential of CQDs is also determined to be 0.22 V vs. RHE (Fig. S5). Accordingly, the Fermi level of CQDs is much lower than that of carbon nitride, thus the electrons in the Fermi level of carbon nitride could easily transfer to the CQDs. As a result, efficient charge separation will be achieved over PCNC-2 catalyst.

To study the separation behavior of photogenerated carriers in the catalysts, the steady state and time-resolved state photoluminescence spectra were collected. In Fig. 5 a, in comparison to PCN, PCNC-2 exhibits a rather weak emission peak at ca. 450 nm originated from electron-hole recombination, implying that the charge recombination in the carbon nitride matrix has been suppressed significantly after being modified with CQDs. In Fig. 5b, the transient photoluminescence result tells that PCNC-2 has a much shorter lifetime than PCN, indicating that the CQDs immobilized in the carbon nitride matrix can trap the photogenerated electrons efficiently [47], thus promotes charge separation and transfer. The photogenerated electrons on CQDs could effectively activate PMS toward organic removal.

The photogenerated charge behavior of the catalysts were further characterized by electrochemical measurement. In Fig. 5c, PCNC-2 presents the Nyquist plots with a much smaller arc radius than PCN both in darkness and with visible-light irradiation, indicative of a remarkable improvement of electrochemical conductivity and interfacial charger transfer ability [60]. Accordingly, PCNC-2 has a higher photocurrent density than PCN (Fig. 5d). These results clearly demonstrate the benefits of incorporating CQDs in carbon nitride matrix for photogenerated charge separation and transfer, which should be rather favorable for PMS activation under visible-light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different oxidation processes for BPA degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic performances of BPA degradation over PCN and PCNC-2 were evaluated in different oxidation processes. In Fig. 6 a, only with visible-light irradiation, the removal rate of BPA is determined to be 10% and 65% in 30 min for Vis/PCN and Vis/PCNC-2 process, respectively. PCNC-2 shows an enhanced photocatalytic activity, which could be ascribed to the enhanced light absorption, enlarged surface area and improved charge separation by CQDs modification. Only in presence of PMS, the removal rate is determined to be 7% in both PMS/PCN and PMS/PCNC-2 process, indicating that little amount of PMS is activated for BPA degradation. In the presence of both visible-light irradiation and PMS, the removal rates significantly increase to 24% and 95% in 30 min over PCN and PCNC-2, respectively. As expected, PCNC-2 exhibits a remarkable enhancement in the performance toward BPA degradation.

The first order-kinetic constant for the different oxidation processes was also calculated and the result is show in Fig. 6b. The Vis-PMS/PCNC-2 process presents a constant of 0.098 min−1, which is 2.6 times higher than the total sum of Vis/PCNC-2 (0.035 min−1) and PMS/PCNC-2 (0.0021 min−1). The synergy factor over PCNC-2 (2.6) is more remarkable than the one over PCN (1.6). This finding evidently demonstrates that PCNC-2 exhibits a rather strong synergy in PMS activation for BPA degradation under visible-light irradiation. In Fig. S6, the TOC removal rate for Vis-PMS/PCNC-2 process is 77% in 30 min, much higher than that for Vis-PMS/PCN process (23%). All these results clearly illustrate the advantages of CQDs modified carbon nitride for photocatalytic activation of PMS for BPA degradation. The influence of the CQDs amount on the catalytic performance of PCNC was also studied and the result is shown in Fig. S7. It is observed that PCNC-2 exhibits the best performance for BPA removal among the catalysts. Further increase of CQDs amount in PCNC causes a decrease in the BPA removal rate, this is mainly due to the light shading effect by excessive CODs. This can be verified by the result of the UV–Vis DRS spectra (Fig. 4a). Furthermore, the comparison between PCNC-2 and the reported photocatalysts were also summarized in Table S1. Even with much lower ratio of [PMS]/[BPA], PCNC-2 displays a good catalytic performance in BPA degradation under visible-light irradiation. In the subsequent parts, PCNC-2 will be adopted for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of reaction parameters on the BPA degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of reaction conditions such as catalyst dosage, PMS concentration, initial pH value and ion additive on BPA degradation by PCNC-2 were also examined. The effect of catalyst dosage is shown in Fig. 7 a, the removal rate of BPA initially increases with the increase of catalyst dosage, and the optimized dosage is 0.33 g/L. When the catalyst dosage further increases to 0.40 g/L, a decrease in the BPA degradation is observed. This could be related to the light-shielding effect by the overdose of catalyst [61], which alleviates the utilization of the incident light for PMS activation. Fig. 7b shows the removal rate of BPA in the presence of different concentrations of PMS (0–40 mg/L). When the PMS concentration increases from 0 to 30 mg/L, the degradation of BPA is accelerated in the presence of PMS with higher concentration. Nevertheless, it approaches saturation when the concentration increases to 40 mg/L. This means that there is a rational match between the photogenerated electrons and PMS concentration and thus generates adequate active species for BPA degradation.

We also investigated the BPA removal rate under the different pH conditions (Fig. 7c), and the optimum catalytic activity is achieved in the initial BPA solution (pH = 6.7). Acid or basic condition is unfavorable for the reaction, especially, high pH (pH = 11) obviously impairs the efficiency of BPA removal. It is generally regarded that the higher pH solution facilitates the activation of PMS and subsequently accelerates the degradation of BPA [62]. The point of zero charge (PZC) of the as-prepared PCNC-2 is measured to be around 6.2 (Fig. S8), which means that the PCNC-2 surface is negatively charged when the solution pH is larger than 7. Thus the electrostatic repulsion between negatively charged PCNC-2 and HSO5 - will affect- the activation of HSO5 – and ROS production, resulting in a poor efficiency of BPA degradation in the high pH solution. In practical application, some common anions such as Cl-, HCO3 –, NO3 – and H2PO4 - would coexist in the sewage, thus potassium chloride, potassium bicarbonate, potassium nitrate or monopotassium phosphate was added to the reaction solution to study its effect on the removal of BPA. In Fig. 7d, the addition of NO3 – or H2PO4 - appears to have an insignificant effect on BPA removal, in comparison to the control experiment. The presence of Cl- slightly improve the BPA degradation rate, which could be due to the generation of reactive chlorine species from the reaction between Cl- and SO4•- [63]. Nevertheless, the presence of HCO3 – significantly suppresses the degradation process and the BPA removal rate is only about 66% in 30 min, this can be explained by that HCO3 – is the scavenger of the active species in the advanced oxidation process [64].

The cyclic test was conducted to study the activity stability of PCNC-2 in the Vis-PMS process. In Fig. 8 a, there is a slight deactivation during the cyclic runs. However, the BPA removal rate still maintains more than 90% in the fourth run. To further explore the physiochemical stability of PCNC-2, the used catalyst was also examined by XRD and FT-IR (Fig. S9). No significant change occurs in crystalline and chemical structure between fresh and used catalyst. Additionally, the catalytic test was also carried out in tap water or lake water to examine the effect of the water quality on BPA degradation in the Vis-PMS/PCNC-2 process. In Fig. 8b, the BPA removal rate in tap water or lake water is similar to that obtained in the deionized water. These results illustrate that PCNC-2 possesses a good reusability for the potential application in the practical wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible reaction mechanism and path of BPA degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The ROS production involved in the photocatalytic activation of PMS was further confirmed by EPR characterizations using DMPO and TMP as the radical spin trapping agents. The produced •OH, SO4•-, O2•- and 1O2 were monitored by the arising of characteristic peaks [65–67]. In Fig. 9 a-c, without a catalyst, the signals assigned to •OH (signal •), SO4•- (signal ♦) O2•- or 1O2 are hardly observed in the Vis-PMS process. Their signals become remarkable and well-resoluble after introducing photocatalyst in the reaction system, clearly demonstrating that PMS is activated by the photocatalytic process. It should be noted that PCNC-2 displays much stronger signals originated from ROS. These results suggest that the incorporation of CQDs in carbon nitride matrix indeed promotes the photocatalytic activation of PMS for producing plentiful ROS, which can well account for its enhanced performance in BPA degradation.

To ascertain the role of the reactive species during the degradation of BPA, different kinds of radical quenchers were added to the reaction system in the Vis-PMS/PCNC-2 process. According the reported, the reaction rate of TBA with •OH is over 100 times faster than that with SO4•-, while MeOH can simultaneously scavenge •OH and SO4•- with similar rate [68–70]. Thus TBA was used as the quencher of •OH, while MeOH was used to eliminate both •OH and SO4•-. Meanwhile, EDTA-Na, FFA and p-BQ were used as scavengers for hole, 1O2 and O2•-, respectively. Fig. 9d shows that the introduction of TBA scarcely affects the BPA removal, while the addition of MeOH slows down the removal rate. The BPA removal rate decreases to less than 30% when the reaction system is introduced with EDTA-Na, FFA or p-BQ. It can be concluded that, 1O2, O2•-, h+, SO4•− and •OH are involved in the reaction system, and 1O2, O2•- and h+ are regarded as the primary ROS in the Vis-PMS/PCNC-2 process for BPA degradation. In the Vis-PMS/PCNC-2 process, sulfate radicals could prefer to react with BPA rather than hydroxyl ion [71], and thus hydroxyl radical contributes little to the Vis-PMS/PCNC-2 process for BPA degradation.
PCNC + hv → eCB -+ hVB + (1)
HSO5 - + eCB - → SO4•- + OH– (2)
O2•- / SO4•- / 1O2 / h+ + BPA → products (5)

On the basis of the above results, a reaction mechanism is proposed and described in (Eqs (1)-(5)) and Scheme 1. Firstly, a certain amount of BPA molecule is adsorbed on the surface of PCNC. Secondly, upon light irradiation, the electrons are excited to the conduction band of the catalyst (eCB -), leaving the holes in the valence band (hVB +) (Eqs (1)). CQDs effectively trap a part of electrons generated in PCNC, suppressing the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Then these electrons react with HSO5 - from PMS and O2 from air to produce O2•-, SO4•-, 1O2 and •OH (Eqs (2)-(4)). Finally, the produced ROS participate in the oxidation of BPA (Eqs (5)).

Intermediate identification has been widely adopted to study the transformation path of BPA, thus LC-MS analysis was carried out to identity the intermediates produced in the Vis-PMS/PCNC-2 process. LC-MS spectra (Fig. S10) show that there are five main by-products with m/z = 244, m/z = 166, m/z = 152, m/z = 134 and m/z = 120. These compounds can be identified as monohydroxylated BPA (MHBPA), 2- (4-oxocyclohexa-2,5-dien-1-yl) propanoic acid, 2-(4-oxocyclohexa-2,5-dien-1-yl) acetic acid, 4-(propan-2-ylidene) cyclohexa-2,5-dien-1-one, and 4-ethylidenecyclohexa-2,5-dienone [72]. Accordingly, a possible pathway for BPA degradation in the Vis-PMS/PCNC-2 process was proposed in Scheme 2 . The BPA degradation comprises of the hydroxylation, chain scission, rearrangement, ring opening processes, and the intermediates can be mineralized by ROS into CO2 and H2O with the prolonged reaction.

In summary, CQDs functionalized carbon nitride was successfully synthesized for photocatalytic activation of PMS toward BPA removal. Benefiting from the unique electronic properties of CQDs, the incorporation of CQDs in carbon nitride matrix exhibits significant advantages for photocatalytic activation of PMS toward BPA removal, including the enhanced light harvesting ability, promoted photogenerated charge separation and abundant active sites for PMS activation. Compared to the pristine PCN, the synthesized PCNC-2 triggered a rather strong synergy in PMS activation for BPA degradation under visible-light irradiation, and it also exhibited a robust stability in cyclic runs. This study provides an example for the development of efficient metal-free photocatalysts for PMS activation toward organic pollutants removal in wastewater.
